title: Tyre-cliff and change-point features from the last 2-5 laps
summary: F1 strategy sources emphasize pit windows, lap-time deterioration, and tyre degradation as dynamic processes, not static ratios.
rationale: F1 strategy sources emphasize pit windows, lap-time deterioration, and tyre degradation as dynamic processes, not static ratios. Recent F1 research also models tyre degradation as a latent evolving state. That points to missed signal in short-horizon slope/acceleration features: the next-lap pit decision should spike when pace loss or degradation abruptly accelerates.
implementation_hint: Within each Year-Race-Driver sequence, compute leakage-safe lagged rolling features: 2/3/5-lap slope of LapTime_Delta, change in Degradation_Per_Lap, second differences, rolling z-scores against same-race-lap peers, positive-delta run length, and simple CUSUM-like accumulated slowdown since the last pit. Feed these to the existing tabular model rather than replacing it.
expected_effect: Moderate upside if pit calls are triggered by recent deterioration bursts rather than absolute tyre age alone; especially useful when compounds or tracks deviate from the hand-set expected-life table.
risk: Easy to break row alignment or leak future laps. Noisy lap times from traffic or wet conditions can make naive rolling features unstable unless split by regime or robustly clipped.
